text: other 17 The person in question must be Durkheim’s contemporary, Camille Jullian (1859–1933, born like Durkheim on April 15). As a historian, philologist and archeologist, Jullian was well-positioned to appreciate the outstanding quality of van Gennep’s works, including those parts of it that touched upon Jullian’s field of expertise, namely antiquity. Whereas van Gennep insisted on liberating ethnography from history, he never showed real history that disdain which would become so marked among Durkheim and his followers. Jullian was a friend of Bergson, often identified as a ‘Liberal’, and just like van Gennep he nurtured a passion for local French history (he wrote the book on the history of Bordeaux still widely used today) and, at the general level, the intertwining of material and spiritual culture. He was Professor at the Collège de France from 1905. But in social situations he was also known to be shy and with a feeble voice, never imposing himself – more or less the opposite of Durkheim. It should also be mentioned that Durkheim had had his struggles with Jullian before. In 1904, when Gabriel Tarde died and Bergson obtained a Chair in Social Philosophy, Durkheim and his collaborators tried hard to transform Bergson’s former chair in Greek and Latin philosophy into a chair in sociology – for which Mauss could then run as a candidate. However, they were voted down, and it was Jullian who was then awarded the chair in ‘History and National Treasures’. Jullian would support Mauss’ candidature in 1907 before that of van Gennep; but by 1915 van Gennep’s merits were so substantial that it had become close to impossible to vote against his candidature. 18 The irony of the irony is this: had van Gennep nurtured German sympathies, it would have done him little harm in Switzerland – perhaps quite the contrary. Instead he publicly denounced the Swiss government for their pro-German attitude via several public letters to the daily, Dépeche de Toulouse. And for that he was forced to return Liminality and the Modern 68 members of the committee, did not consider his works of any great value. His candidature was turned down. It seems that van Gennep was never again taken up in the correspondence between Durkheim and Mauss. It must be stressed that while we may lack further direct proof of Durkheim’s role in preventing van Gennep’s access to the job market, the situation must be put in its general context. Durkheim had held a chair’s position at the Sorbonne since 1902, but he had also built up a much wider power base. Some of Durkheim’s enemies would openly accuse him of ‘managing’ appointments and creating chairs of sociology in provincial universities in order to extend his influence (see Jones 1986: 12–23). Leaving aside the various aspects of the situation that will probably remain conjectural, the fact remains that one of France’s most promising social scientists did not even manage to land a teaching position at a minor provincial university. And so, with 20 languages spoken and twice the number read, a handful of the most important social science books translated from English into French, with twelve books published together with around 100 articles and reviews, directing a fairly successful journal of ethnography and sociology, with administrative skills from a position within a French ministry, with practical skills in museology, with fieldwork experience in Algeria and sustained ethnographic data-gathering in several French provinces, having just recently, as the first person ever, assembled 600 of Europe’s most promising names in ethnography and anthropology to discuss the raison d’être and future of their discipline, leading what would for decades remain the biggest anthropological conference ever held, by the end of 1915 Arnold van Gennep would return to France empty-handed. And unemployed. On the Parallels between Gabriel Tarde and Arnold Van Gennep To suggest strong parallels between the work of Arnold van Gennep and Gabriel Tarde may seem both unnecessary, forced and anachronistic. And in many ways, they are of course not comparable. Tarde and van Gennep were not close friends or allies. They belonged to different generations and only lived in Paris at the same time for a few years, from 1901 when van Gennep returned from Poland to Paris until 1904 when Tarde died – and even then they apparently did not visit the same circles. Gabriel Tarde had a wildly successful career, crowned in 1900 with the Chair of modern philosophy at the Collège de France (much to Durkheim’s anger); he was 15 years older than Durkheim. Arnold van Gennep was instead 15 years younger than Durkheim, had a different background, was interested in different areas of research than those of Tarde, and never even held a position in France. And yet the parallels are strikingly strong, and for four reasons that we can quickly spell out (for further detail, see Thomassen 2012 c). The purpose is, again, not to his homeland France – where he would once again be put under suspicion for being potentially anti-patriotic. Arnold van Gennep and his Contemporaries 69 just a matter of intellectual history: what is at stake is an attempt to re-establish foundations from which to analyse social phenomena in ways that are meaningful. First, and most evidently, even if Tarde had a successful career, he was eventually sidelined by the Durkheimian school, and in a way even more radically so than Arnold van Gennep, who after all ‘survived’ as a folklorist. This has much to do with the fact that Tarde committed the unforgivable error of dying in 1904, just as Durkheim was about to secure his stronghold. Tarde’s writings were dismissed and literally taken out of the sociological curriculum. For decades – in fact, for most of a century – they only survived in America, via the Chicago school of sociology. Second, what unites Tarde and van Gennep is that they were unlucky enough to hold knowledge and methodological expertise in exactly those areas that Durkheim was trying to claim ‘his own’, and which he programmatically used
